author: aleabitoreddit
text:
Just from my personal experiences:

My new heuristic is markets tend to price things in ~8 months in advance unless your theme is Space, Robotics/Quantum.

For themes I'm tracking:

For photonics:
- Next-gen pluggables (1.6T) are right around now (ex. $LITE, $COHR, Innolight, etc.) then $AAOI, $JBL 1.6T LRO and other later bloomers H1 2027.
- CPO Scale Out related stuff is late H2 2026 (ex. $POET, Shunsin, etc)
- NPO stuff H2 2026/2027 prob as bridge architectures
- CPO Scale Up H2 2027 (ex. Ayar, Celestial, etc).

If I use the 8 month rule like 4 months too early for CPO Scale Up Names.

But in hindsight, I wanted to be early this time around given I felt this was the most compelling architectural shift given GS tam projections + markets seem to disagree, which is why there's a selloff with Foci/MSScorp and others.

Maybe it should start picking up again and start to be priced in December 2026 IG? idk we'll see.

Stuff like lasers, $AXTI (InP substrates) are used across all those themes. FAU/silicon photonics/PCB midplanes, etc. are more concentrated around different architectures.

Memory theme is quite a massive bottleneck as you've been seeing:
- HBM3e around now as you've seen with Micron, Samsung, Sk Hynix etc. and NAND with Sandisk.
- HBM4 mass production right around now too with SK Hynix, Micron/Samsung.
- HBF 2027?

But again these are the same Kioxia, Samsung, Sk Hynix, $MU, Kioxia players across different architectures, and we're seeing re-rating cause we're mid-bottleneck. So these are likely to keep compounding.

Glass Substrates:
H2 2026 start with SKC Absolics (probably just early stage ramp). H2 2027-2028 is where the fun begins, with 2028 TSM CoPoS, others like Samsung etc. 2027, and Intel later on.

Humanoid Suppliers:
Very early, but as I said with Space markets probably won't wait to price this in with Elon giving bullish $TSLA optimus projections and Chinese humanoid companies like Unitree/AGI Bot starting to ramp up.

Neoclouds:
I said this was probably the buildout period for many of the players and Q4 2026 earnings is when some of the leaders typically hit inflection. Maybe was a bit early focusing so hard H4 2025 last year. Like with $NBIS $7-9B ARR, that ARR proj should hit Q4 earnings. If we apply that ~8M rule, Jan/Feb was very depressing, but should have bought in March 2026 rather than Sept last year. But markets might be pricing in Feb 2027 window.

So I'm personally probably going to repositioning right now around: Memory, Neoclouds, Photonics, Robotics. Probably heavier when something is ~8m in advance or captures many different themes eg. $SIVE for pluggable, NPO, CPO, and all architectures. And just having exposure to mini-shifts in between like advanced packaging materials, hybrid bonding for memory, or random bottlenecks like MLCCs.

I probably shouldn't have heavy exposure to something way out 12M+ if I rethink my decisions. Also something to note is machine suppliers in general hit revenue faster than the general volume ramp, with your main companies like Samsung, Absolics, etc. need machines to get going in the first place. Which is why I ended up getting $LPK positions personally.

I should follow my personal 8 rule to the T myself since I probably was a little too early to many names. Those are just my own experiences tho I'm learning along the way.

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
Just some random notes : 

The upstream MLCC bottleneck stack looks like this:

MLCC-grade BaTiO₃ / dielectric powder
- Sakai Chemical (40%), Nippon Chemica, Fuji Titanium / MF Material, Sinocera

kinda important too:
high purity Ni powder and Ni internal-electrode paste
- Shoei Chemical, JFE Mineral, Toho Titanium, Sumitomo Metal Mining, Daiken Chemical

Other:
Dy/Y/Ho/Gd rare-earth dopants and high-purity oxide additives
- Solvay (purity oxide additives/dopants), Shin‑Etsu, Mitsui Mining, Lynas Rare Earths, etc.

misc: 

silicon capacitors- AP memory, SEMCO, murata just looking into them vs. mlcc

PMICs (TI, Infineon, Renesas, Analog Devices, MPS, ON Semi, NXP)

BMCs - Aspeed: ~$18.5B MC: BMCs. around 70% global BMC share (very interesting)

Then China indium metal export controls kinda like Wf6 if anything goes down:

- InGaAs, InAs, InSb, InGaP, AlInGaP, InGaN, infrared detectors, LEDs, VCSELs, CPV, sensors, epitaxy source materials, and MOCVD precursors.

Non-China refined indium producers: Korea Zinc/Young Poong, DOWA/Mitsui/JX/Sumitomo Japan type companies, Teck obvious just substitute trade 

Stockpile suppliers: eg. companies sitting on a bunch, kinda like RPI hoarding memory then benefiting later on

-ITO substitutes: (antimony tin oxide, carbon nanotubes, PEDOT conductive polymers, silver/copper nanowires, graphene, and zinc oxide nanopowder)?

cause Indium Tin Oxide is largest volume use of indium. 

so smartphone, TV, and automotive display manufacturing might have troubles lol. 

Already covered China export controls quite a bit with InP substrates and 
$AXTI
, I'm just trying to guess what happens with indium metal export controls next. 

Idk yet still looking

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
So I’m reading about an activist report about WUS (2316) right now from Palliser.

I actually thought their report was super compelling… that I decided to follow along and buy in as well. (not my idea this time, I own shares, had to change some weightings around to add this in).

Basically WUS Taiwan owns 11.3% of Kunshan (which is 45% Nvidia AI switch PCB share from their ests.).

WUS TW is valued ~$1B, probably from their own standalone PCB business, which does around $120M revenue, with AI growth ahead.

That stake of WUS Kunshan is worth around $4-4.3B MC.

After tax it’s around $3.2B, which by itself is 3-4x the current business value.

And they’re trying to pressure management to unlock that value.

I looked at WUS for PCB exposure earlier, and thought it was an interesting idea but didn’t know about ownership.

And this should derisk downside quite a bit. 

Main risk is that is management ignores them completely, but don’t think it will be completely ignored like Snapchat. 

Maybe some middle ground like selling 1/3rd stake?

Apparently it’s some family business that’s stubborn but feel like activists can do some work.

Anyway just doing research into this now sharing my findings.

Sk square’s around more than half NAV of Sk Hynix ownership, eg. $138b to $235b.

I thought this was more unique since nav disc was more extreme $1b to $4.1B.

And underlying PCB business anyway is set to take off from ai growth. I was looking at this before without considering their underlying assets.

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
So doing research into some random 
$TSM
, SK Hynix, SMIC, type bottleneck. 

not familiar with WF₆ but Peric Special Gases (SHA: 688146) is main benefiary. 

TLDR: China -> Tungsten export controls (60-70% of Wf6 costs)
-> Needed for DRAM/NAND
-> Japan (35% of capacity)
-> South Korea 20% of cpacity

"Japan’s Kanto Denka Kogyo and Central Glass had warned South Korean  chipmakers such as Samsung Electronics and DB HiTek of potential WF₆  supply disruptions"

ChemNet reports that Kanto Denka and Central Glass are expected to permanently halt production starting July 2026.

"WF₆ is a critical precursor gas applied in DRAM, NAND,"

Peric Special Gases entered the supply chains of leading global foundries and memory makers, including 
$TSM
, Samsung, SMIC.

So usually you can just invest in the main 
$AXTI
 type beneficary... 

Or go the other route of looking at other players that can make up for supply gaps in western supply chains

I was doing too much research into inp substrates that i forgot about Tungsten third order effects i guess... 

By the time news publishes something, kinda on the later end but helpful to see if there's any ripple effect anywhere


Serenity

@aleabitoreddit
·
OP
·
Jun 12
$AXTI
↗

Complicated, 
$AXTI
, Peric and others are more priced in now, so upside is still there but a little less. 

I'm just sharing a framework on how to find future bottlenecks (12-jun)

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
Samsung/SK Hynix Supplier Korean Stock Idea:

It's kinda like $SIVE + CPO mixed with 
$AEHR
 but for memory (and less TAM).

Auros (322310): ~$210m MC.

It's the SK Hynix and Samsung supplier for next-gen HBM.

This is a direct play for HBM4e hybrid bonding (every memory maker) + HBM4 (samsung) / metrology.

A comparison is $SIVE for CPO, where they weren't really around for  HBM3 (photonics equivalent is like 800g) and have been developing the  past many years. But with next-gen it's there for volume ramp.

It's the only Korean Domestic company for IR metrology. And product #1 is used for hybrid bonding.

So Samsung HBM4 lines use hybrid copper bonding in 2026, so they're trying to push overlay tool through qualification H2?

Sk Hynix actually just used MR-MUF (no hybrid bonding) for HBM4. But  Trendforce reported today SK Hynix has completed 12-high hybrid  bonding HBM validation and is raising yields for mass production?

So maybe SK Hynix are adopters too soon, which is bullish. 

But at least for short-term it's probably Samsung first for volume if qualifications pass.

$KLA is a pure monopoly over this sort of IR metrology overlay that Korea (SK Hynix/Samsung) wants to break.

So Samsung they are likely using Auros to customize IR tools and keep domestic secrets.

Think of it as the Korean 
$INTC
 where they will use their domestic  champion as much as possible if it actually works. And both will ramp up  HBM4E -> HBM5 with this technology, so multiple years capex.

For product #2 (this is why I mention 
$AEHR
, similar):

There's also the "AUROS Technology Inc. is conducting qualification  testing of its ultra-precision thin-film thickness measurement equipment  with a major  domestic chipmaker".

This was dominated by $KLA and 
$AMAT
 before.

"Development began at its research center, followed by pilot   manufacturing at a new facility from 2021, with about four years  required to reach the current qualification stage"

Basically they've been developing many years + qualifying now, and  around h2 if it passes it switches to high volume production for 2027.

So this is the sweet spot for timelines. It's really high risktho ...

But high reward, lower downside risk given Samsung/SK Hynix are kinda supporting this company. (disclosure: have positions, NFA, DYOR)

Anyway, still doing reserach into this but found it compelling, just my thoughts so far.

TLDR:

Not really well known $210M MC supplier to Samsung/SK Hynix.

Then both of their main product lines finish qualification h2 for  volume ramp 2027? So h1 into h2 (now) seemed like the sweet spot.

Still doing research of course, new company to me. just caught my eye. Very very early of course before any movement.

 Which is really good given TAM expansion optionality what

 is doing? I preferred Coherent for now, but might give this another look. Hard to go wrong with Jabil?

$COHR
 or split it. 

2. IntelliEPI looking into it got interested for inp epitaxial stuff and they got chips act subsidies recently. didn't get enough time yet. 

3. $SMHD - i passed last yr bc of toxic financing reasons, when i was looking at glass substrate stuff, but decided to take another look.

q1-$30m convertible notes, so that's dilution overhang, not that bad.
F1 resale: 46.6M new share issuance, with registered up to 95M share resale
So that's roughly $563m off $303m MC?

Like they're probably supplying to 
$AMAT
, Ibiden, Unimicron and these types of customers as a fundamental supplier but not sure if it outweights dilution? 

4. Was looking at quantum dot lasers / BTO again vs. ELS and others... Way, way too early like with names like QD laser or 
$ALMU
 should prob come back 1 year later.

I did like QD laser more since it's funded/spinoff of Fujitsu, so you probably won't get that dilution nuke that 
$POET

That being said there's some interesting names for sure, might just take a stake in one or two quantum names just in case there's a breakthrough. 

5. Then looking more into Furukawa and 
$AVGO
 relationships out of curiosity. they have massive manufacturing scale eg. ~500% expansion from 2025 capex.  So if there's ever a bottleneck for the lasers in 2027-2028 just remember Furukawa, Win semi, and others will benefit. 

But i dont think it will be as severe as the EML bottleneck? who knows. 

6. Probably will look into Yuanjie next, kinda china's photonics program's leader that supplies to innolight and others i think. from first pass, looks like a compelling long? But I wouldnt invest personally unless it was like Unitree level undervalued. 

Don't have any positions in these names, just dropping some thoughts in case its useful.

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
So my opinion on current markets and thoughts:

Lot of photonics stuff ran up too much like 
$LITE
, 
$AXTI
, 
$AAOI
, and others. eg. 
$AAOI
 $40 -> $150. 

Probably going to see some range-bound chopping for awhile until next earnings? 

 While a lot of the newer CPO ecosystem names like ASE, Sivers, etc, have a lot of re-rating to do. 

Stuff like 
$AEHR
 more priced in after the 100% rally, probably better not to take short term capital gains imo... I can see it chopping a tiny bit too. But hint was volume orders shortly after. So maybe if you wait a month, volume order hits, then stock goes up another 30% is my guess? 

There's some nuance like 
$SMTC
 for LPO/LRO transitions, but it's really complicated as a briedge. 

Software obviously got sold off too much. I was questioning why 
$META
, 
$MSFT
 were sold off 20% after macro, even more with 
$RDDT
.  

Even though Meta was doing 36% or so revenue growth Y/Y, which is stupidly insane. And reddit was doing 1/3 net income of revenue that grew 70% Y/Y. 

I think AI is accelerates revenue for some software names actually, esp. social media. 

But there's obvious companies that get disrupted over time as seen with Chegg from both vibe coded competitors as well as just direct to AI from ChatGPT/Gemini. 

As for Crypto, got sold off too much. I'm always bullish Ethereum 1.5-2K, and bearish at 3-4k. Don't ask me why but it just repeats like this for the past 6 years.

I like Bitcoin, and some protocols that actually generate revenue like hyperliquid. 

$CRCL
 is extreme nuanced. Clarity act is a nuke on yields, and I can't project how that would affect circulating supply yet. 

Rate cuts projections dropped to 0 as highest probability. Which is heavily bullish circle, but if yields get banned, idk if stablecoin derivative liqudity flows into USDC supply or if liquidity just gets nuked if people just convert stablecoins to just banks instead. 

I don't have the answer. 

Stuff that I said early H1, like 
$MRVL
, 
$NBIS
 being accelerated latter part of H2 is playing out now. Nebius was Q4 earnings (the big showtime) onward. 

Marvell benefits so much from ASIC revenue like Microsoft Maia (which I've heard rumors about delay), and there's some rumors about them design wins from 
$GOOGL
 TPU but I think that's just to scare Broadcom/mediatek into better contracts. 

They actually lose off from CPO, but have Celestial, the business is just way too * big so really hard to model. But it seems like a good compounder. 

Stuff like 
$INTC
, 
$ARM
 great holds. Just generally, Intel thesis still stands (made in america) for foundry. 

$ARM
 unholy revenue acceleration from CPUs. And as you might have seen theres a CPU shortage too. 

As for memory companies... My 
$EWY
 leaps hit triple digit returns, I'm just holding. 

Not seeing any red flags, I already said the Google algo paper was stupid and won't affect DRAM/NAND prices much. And if you read it, it optimized lighter models not larger frontier models. 

I did cut crypto exposure to chase AI semi supply chain names, but I do expect Bitcoin to recover... And if does, 
$HOOD
, and other companies heavily tied to crypto revenue will do well too.  

SpaceX IPO at $1.75T is just insane... idk. 
$RKLB
 was my long back at $10's, but it's just extreme premiums at $40B. They're not going to make much revenue from Neutron until like 2028+, but markets don't care. 

I've said it before but Rocketlab is my favorite long long term stock, but you'll probably get faster returns elsewhere. 

I don't think capex/revenue driving SpaceX will funnel down into the supply chains to the degree of AI. 

The valuations look insane, but capex is not anywhere close to the degree of frontier lab spend. But still doing more research here. 

As for overall macro, I wouldn't fade this rally and just go along for the ride.

Markets are extremely resilient and midterms are now coming up and it would look super bad, if markets were very red. 

What I'm doing is:

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
Then long pure play 1.6T/CPO ramp has highest upside. 

Have some misc stocks for quantum, and others.

$TSEM
, etc. even if it chops a bit, more better to not take short term capital gains imo? even if it's more priced in can keep going higher.

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
Robotics is next. 

Both deal count and investment amounts are skyrocketing per pitchbook March data (source: a16z)

Good thing is: the same AI DC exposure often has cross-exposure to humanoid ramp.

Like DRAM/NAND with memory (on humanoid inference/storage) or DFB lasers with photonics (FMCW LiDAR vision/sensing).

Right now most exposure is upstream component parts… or programs within large players like $AMZN or $TSLA. 

So global IPO season H2 into 2027 for pure play humanoids/robotics companies is going to be fun. 

Robotics investment hits all time high
